content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

October 29, 1996

Cyril F. Kellett, M.D.
3927 Waring Road, Suite A
Oceanside, California 92056

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-268

Dear Dr. Kellett:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 concerning
statements of economic interests.

QUESTION

How are you required to report income received by your
medical practice on your statement of economic interests?

CONCLUSION

As discussed below, you are required to report income that
you receive from Cyril F. Kellett M.D., Inc., a corporation that
is owned by you and your wife. You are also required to report
entities or individuals who are sources of income to that
corporation if your pro rata share of the income is $10,000 or
greater.

FACTS

You are a member of the Tri-City Hospital District Board. As
a boardmember, you file statements of economic interests under the
Act. You report income from Cyril F. Kellett, M.D., Inc., a
corporation owned by you and your wife. You ask how income from
your medical practice should be reported on your statement of
economic interests, and whether you have to list every patient as
a source of income.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations
appear at Title 2, Sections 18000-18995 of the California Code of
Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-268
Page 2


     Doctors on the medical staff at the Tri-City Hospital have a
corporation called the Tri-City Independent Physicians Association
(Tri-City IPA"). You are affiliated with the Tri-city IPA.  The
Tri-City IPA is an administrative unit that handles third party
contracts with Tri-City IPA physicians.  Approximately 50 or more
third party payers (i.e., various health maintenance organizations
and health plans) pay Cyril F. Kellett, M.D., Inc.

                              ANALYSIS

     1.  Reporting Income From Your Professional Corporation.  A
public official who is designated in a conflict of interest code
is required to disclose sources of income on his or her statement
of economic interests.  (Sections 87300, 87302, and 87207.)  As a
member of the Tri-City Hospital Board, you are a public official
who must file statements of economic interests.  You ask how to
report income from your medical practice on your statement of
economic interests.

     You must report on Schedule D, "Income," your pro rata share
of the gross receipts of Cyril F. Kellett, M.D., Inc. Under the
Act, an official's income includes a community property interest
in the income of their spouse.  (Section 82030(a).)  For reporting
purposes, your pro rata share of income from Cyril F. Kellett
M.D., Inc., is equal to the percentage of the business that you
own plus one-half of your wife's interest.  You stated that you
and your wife each have a one-half ownership interest in Cyril F.
Kellett, M.D., Inc.  Therefore, your pro rata share of income
received by the corporation would be 75 percent of the total.

     Under the Act, an individual's income also includes a pro
rata share of the income of any business entity in which the
individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a
10-percent or greater interest.  (Section 82030(a).)  You and your
spouse are the sole owners of Cyril F. Kellett, M.D., Inc.
Because you own more than a 10-percent interest in this
professional corporation, you must report sources of income to the
corporation where your pro rata share is $10,000 or more per year.

     Section 87207 of the Act requires as follows:

                            * * *

     (b)  When the filer's pro rata share of income
to a business entity, including income to a sole
proprietorship, is required to be reported under
this article, the statement shall contain:

          (1)  The name, address, and a general
          description of the business activity of the
          business entity;

File No. A-96-268
Page 3


                 (2)  The name of every person2 from whom the
        business entity received payments if the filer's
        pro rata share of gross receipts from such person
        was equal to or greater than ten thousand dollars
        ($10,000) during a calendar year.

                                    * * *

                        Section 87207(b).
                        (Emphasis added.)

        You must report patients or health plans3 that are sources of
income to your medical corporation if your pro rata share of the
income is $10,000 or greater.  Report income to your corporation
on Schedule H-2, "Income and Loans to a Business Entity."

        For example, suppose a health plan or a patient makes a
$20,000 payment to Cyril F. Kellett, M.D., Inc.  Because you and
your spouse each own a 50 percent interest in Cyril F. Kellett,
M.D., Inc., your pro rata share of the payment would be $10,000,
plus one-half of your spouse's interest, $5,000, totalling
$15,000.  (Eads Advice Letter, No. A-82-112; and Bloom-Rudibaugh
Advice Letter, No. A-94-399.)  You must report any payments of
$13,333 or more made by health plans, patients, or other entities
to your corporation.4

        Correspondence from the Tri-City Hospital District's attorney
to you questions whether a check from the Tri-City IPA to "Cyril
Kellett MD" dated August 14, 1995, was income directed to you in
your personal capacity, rather than income to your professional
corporation, raising additional reporting obligations.  You state
that this check was to your professional corporation and was
deposited in that account.  As income to your professional
corporation, payments from the Tri-City IPA do not need to be




_________________________
2          Person is broadly defined to include individuals,
corporations, nonprofit organizations, and other entities.
(Section 82047.)

3          Income to you or your professional corporation from sources
that are outside the Tri-City Hospital District's jurisdiction and
are not doing business in the jurisdiction are not reportable.
(Section 82030(a).)

4          Suppose a health plan or patient makes a $13,333 payment to
Cyril F. Kellett, M.D., Inc.  As you and your spouse each own a
50-percent interest in the corporation, your pro rata share of the
payment would be $6,667, plus one-half of your spouse's interest,
$3,333, which equals $10,000.

File No. A-96-268
Page 4


reported separately on schedule H-2 unless they equal $13,333 or
more in a calendar year.5

    2.  Physician-Patient Privilege.  If a patient makes a
payment of $13,333 or more to Cyril F. Kellett, M.D., Inc., you
must report the patient's name on your statement of economic
interests unless it is protected by the physician-patient
privilege.  Under the Act, a patient's name is protected by the
physician-patient privilege only when disclosure of the patient's
name would also reveal the nature of the treatment received by the
patient.  Under these circumstances, you may decline to report a
client's name if you believe that the name is protected by the
physician-patient privilege.  However, you must file an
explanation for such nondisclosure with your statement of economic
interests.  With respect to each undisclosed patient, you must
state that to the best of your knowledge you have not or will not
make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use your
official position to influence a governmental decision when to do
so would constitute a violation of the Act's conflict of interest
provisions.  (Regulation 18740, copy enclosed.)

    3.  Amending a Statement of Economic Interests.  If you need
to amend a statement of economic interest for a prior year, you
may use an amendment schedule (copy enclosed).  You must fill out
a separate amendment and verification for each year that
amendments are necessary.  (Please note that the Commission's
legal division does not provide advice regarding past conduct.
Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct
that has already taken place.)

    4.  Conflicts of Interest and Disqualification.  The
Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.  (Section 87100.)  A public
official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate
family, or on the official's business, property, sources of
income, or other economic interest.

    You may not make or participate in making decisions of the
Tri-City Hospital Board that would have a material financial
effect on Cyril F. Kellett, M.D., Inc., or on the health plans,
patients, or other entities that are sources of income to that
corporation where your pro rata share of the income is $250 or
more per year.  (See Lofholm Advice Letter, No. A-96-019, copy

_________________________
5  You have not provided enough facts for us to determine whether
the Tri-City IPA or the Secure Horizons/University of California
San Diego contract is the reportable source of income in this
case.

File No. A-96-268
Page 5


enclosed.)  You should review the enclosed copy of a guide to the
Act's conflict-of-interest provisions for public officials.

     I trust this answers your question.  If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 916/322-
5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Hyla P. Wagner
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

SGC:HPW:ak
